title: SEGMENT 10 — Chemotherapy pharmacogenomics: TPMT, DPYD, and UGT1A1

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — Chemotherapy pharmacogenomics: TPMT, DPYD, and UGT1A1
Welcome back.

Now I want to spend a segment on a corner of pharmacogenomics where the testing is, by current consensus, simply mandatory before prescribing — a corner where the science is so well-established, and the consequences of getting it wrong so severe, that essentially no responsible clinician initiates these drugs without testing.
That corner is the metabolism of certain chemotherapy and chemotherapy-adjacent drugs.
Let's start with the cleanest example.
6-mercaptopurine and the enzyme TPMT.
6-mercaptopurine, often abbreviated 6-MP, and its closely related cousin azathioprine, are thiopurine drugs.
They've been around since the 1950s.

They're used in the maintenance phase of childhood leukemia — particularly acute lymphoblastic leukemia, ALL, the most common childhood cancer — and they're used as immunosuppressants for autoimmune conditions like inflammatory bowel disease, after organ transplants, and so on.
The mechanism is that thiopurines get incorporated into DNA in dividing cells, where they interfere with cell division.
In leukemia, this kills the malignant lymphoblasts.
In autoimmune disease, this suppresses proliferating immune cells.
To do their job, thiopurines have to be metabolized into active intracellular metabolites.
They also get inactivated by competing pathways, the most important of which is methylation by an enzyme called thiopurine S-methyltransferase, TPMT.
Here's the thing.
TPMT activity varies dramatically across the population, and the variation is largely genetic.
About 1 in 300 people is TPMT-deficient — they carry two no-function alleles, the most common being TPMT\3A or TPMT\3C.
In a TPMT-deficient patient, the inactivation pathway is gone.
The thiopurine drug, given at a standard dose, accumulates the active intracellular metabolite to ten or twenty times the normal level.
The result is profound, life-threatening bone marrow suppression.
White blood cells, red blood cells, platelets — all wiped out.
The patient becomes severely immunocompromised, anemic, and at risk of bleeding.
In a child being treated for leukemia, this can be fatal within days.
The intermediate metabolizers — about 10% of the population, with one no-function allele — also experience excess toxicity at standard doses, though less severe.
The testing infrastructure here is mature and the dosing adjustments are precise.
Patients identified as TPMT poor metabolizers get dramatically reduced thiopurine doses — sometimes as low as 10% of the standard dose.
Intermediate metabolizers get something like 30-70% of the standard dose.
Normal metabolizers get the full dose.
With proper dosing, all three groups can be treated safely and effectively.

In pediatric oncology — childhood leukemia treatment — TPMT genotyping (and increasingly, NUDT15 genotyping, which is particularly important in Asian populations) before thiopurine therapy is standard of care.
It's no longer optional.
Every major pediatric oncology center in the developed world tests every patient before they start 6-MP, and adjusts the dose to genotype.
This is one of the success stories.
Now let me do two more in quick succession.
Irinotecan and UGT1A1\*28.** Irinotecan is a chemotherapy drug used for colorectal cancer and some others.
Its active metabolite is detoxified by an enzyme called UGT1A1, which adds a glucuronic acid group and makes the molecule water-soluble for excretion.

Patients with a variant called UGT1A1\28 — a polymorphism in the gene's promoter, which by the way is also responsible for Gilbert's syndrome, a benign condition causing mild hyperbilirubinemia — have reduced UGT1A1 activity.
They detoxify irinotecan more slowly.
At standard doses, they have substantially elevated rates of severe diarrhea and neutropenia, both of which can be life-threatening.
The FDA recommends genotype-guided dose reduction in UGT1A1\28 homozygotes.
5-fluorouracil and DPYD.
5-FU is one of the most commonly used chemotherapy drugs in the world — backbone of treatment for colorectal, breast, and several other cancers.
It's metabolized and inactivated by an enzyme called dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase, DPYD.
About 3-8% of the population carries a partial-deficiency variant, and a very small fraction (~0.2%) are essentially DPYD-deficient.
A DPYD-deficient patient given a standard 5-FU dose can die — and has died — from overwhelming toxicity.
Severe mucositis, neutropenia, cardiotoxicity, neurotoxicity.
There are documented cases of patients receiving their first 5-FU infusion and dying within two weeks from the drug.
In 2020, after a series of fatal cases and a successful trial, DPYD genotyping became mandatory before 5-FU treatment across much of Europe.
The United States has been slower to mandate it, but most major cancer centers now do it routinely.
The drug has saved millions of lives, but it has also killed people who could have been identified by a $100 genetic test done before the first infusion.
Step back and look at the pattern.
Three chemotherapy or chemotherapy-related drugs — thiopurines, irinotecan, 5-FU.
Three different enzymes — TPMT, UGT1A1, DPYD.
Three different genetic variants.

But the same underlying phenomenon: a fraction of patients with reduced metabolic clearance, who at standard doses develop life-threatening toxicity, and who can be identified by a cheap, fast, one-time genetic test before the first dose.
This is what mature, clinically-implemented pharmacogenomics looks like.
The science is settled.
The guidelines are clear.
The infrastructure exists.
The patients are tested.
Lives are saved.
There's no debate.
The remaining question, for these and other drug-gene pairs, is implementation — how do we make sure every patient who needs the test gets it, at every hospital, in every country?
And that's the question we'll spend the next several segments on.
